 {"id":"bd-1d7n","title":"[10.13] Implement deterministic activation pipeline: sandbox -> ephemeral secret mount -> capability issue -> health-ready transition.","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md
Section: 10.13 — FCP Deep-Mined Expansion Execution Track (9I)

Why This Exists:
Deep connector/provider contract track: lifecycle FSMs, conformance harnesses, fencing, sandboxing, revocation freshness, and protocol hardening.

Task Objective:
Implement deterministic activation pipeline: sandbox -> ephemeral secret mount -> capability issue -> health-ready transition.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Stage order is fixed and enforced; partial activation cannot leak persistent secrets; restart replay reproduces identical activation transcript.

Expected Artifacts:
- `docs/specs/activation_pipeline.md`, `tests/integration/activation_pipeline_determinism.rs`, `artifacts/10.13/activation_stage_transcript.jsonl`.

- Machine-readable verification artifact at `artifacts/section_10_13/bd-1d7n/verification_evidence.json` for CI/release gating.
- Human-readable verification summary at `artifacts/section_10_13/bd-1d7n/verification_summary.md` linking implementation intent to measured outcomes.
Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for core logic, invariants, and error handling.
- Integration/E2E tests for end-to-end control-flow behavior.
- Detailed structured logs/telemetry with stable codes and trace correlation IDs.
- Deterministic replay fixture or reproducible artifact for failure analysis.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- For "[10.13] Implement deterministic activation pipeline: sandbox -> ephemeral secret mount -> capability issue -> health-ready transition.", preserve full capability scope from the plan; no feature compression or silent omission is allowed.
- Verification for "[10.13] Implement deterministic activation pipeline: sandbox -> ephemeral secret mount -> capability issue -> health-ready transition." must include capability-specific thresholds and machine-readable pass/fail evidence artifacts.
- Unit tests, integration/E2E scripts, and structured logs for "[10.13] Implement deterministic activation pipeline: sandbox -> ephemeral secret mount -> capability issue -> health-ready transition." must support deterministic replay and root-cause triage without hidden context.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- "[10.13] Implement deterministic activation pipeline: sandbox -> ephemeral secret mount -> capability issue -> health-ready transition." is required to reduce operator error, increase deterministic safety guarantees, and improve time-to-diagnosis for failures in real-world workflows.
- Explicit success/failure evidence for "[10.13] Implement deterministic activation pipeline: sandbox -> ephemeral secret mount -> capability issue -> health-ready transition." prevents false confidence and improves trust in migration/compatibility/security outcomes.
- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:52.865799544Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T11:52:50.417718334Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T11:52:50.417692176Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-13","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1d7n","depends_on_id":"bd-3i9o","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:13.012177086Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1dar","title":"[10.14] Implement optional MMR checkpoints and inclusion/prefix proof APIs for external verifiers.","description":"## Why This Exists

Merkle Mountain Range (MMR) checkpoints and proof APIs allow external verifiers to independently verify that specific markers are included in the marker stream and that a given stream is a valid prefix of another, without requiring access to the full stream. This is critical for the 9J trust model where external auditors, federated nodes, or compliance systems need to verify control-plane integrity without being trusted with the full marker history. MMR proofs provide compact, O(log N) inclusion and prefix proofs that can be verified with only the MMR root and the proof path. This bead directly supports runtime invariant #8 (evidence-by-default: verifiable proofs exist for all control events) and #9 (deterministic verification gates: proofs are machine-checkable). The 10.10 rollback/fork detection system (bd-2ms) uses these proofs for cross-node verification.

## What This Must Do

1. Implement `MmrCheckpoint` in `src/control_plane/mmr_proofs.rs` that maintains an MMR accumulator over the marker stream, updated on each marker append.
2. Implement `mmr_inclusion_proof(stream: &MarkerStream, checkpoint: &MmrCheckpoint, seq: u64) -> Result<InclusionProof>` that generates a compact proof that marker at `seq` is included in the stream up to the checkpoint.
3. Implement `mmr_prefix_proof(checkpoint_a: &MmrCheckpoint, checkpoint_b: &MmrCheckpoint) -> Result<PrefixProof>` that proves checkpoint_a is a valid prefix of checkpoint_b (i.e., the stream at checkpoint_a is an initial segment of the stream at checkpoint_b).
4. Implement `verify_inclusion(proof: &InclusionProof, root: &MmrRoot, marker_hash: &Hash) -> Result<(), ProofError>` and `verify_prefix(proof: &PrefixProof, root_a: &MmrRoot, root_b: &MmrRoot) -> Result<(), ProofError>` for external verifiers.
5. MMR checkpoints must be optional and togglable: enabling/disabling MMR does not corrupt the underlying marker stream. When disabled, proof APIs return `MmrDisabled` error.
6. Publish proof vectors: a JSON file containing `(stream_markers, target_seq, mmr_root, inclusion_proof, expected_verification_result)` tuples for external implementations.
7. Produce verifier examples that demonstrate proof generation and verification end-to-end.

## Acceptance Criteria

- MMR checkpoints can be enabled/disabled without corrupting marker truth; proof APIs verify inclusion and prefix claims; verifier examples pass.
- Inclusion proof for any marker in a 10,000-marker stream verifies successfully against the MMR root.
- Inclusion proof for a marker NOT in the stream fails verification deterministically.
- Prefix proof between two checkpoints (stream at 5,000 and stream at 10,000) verifies successfully.
- Disabling MMR checkpoints does not affect marker stream read/write operations.
- Re-enabling MMR checkpoints rebuilds the accumulator from the existing stream correctly.
- Published proof vectors contain at least 10 inclusion and 5 prefix test cases.
- Proof size is O(log N) for a stream of N markers.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests: inclusion proof generation and verification for first, last, and middle markers; invalid inclusion proof rejection; prefix proof generation and verification; MMR disable/enable cycle; accumulator rebuild from existing stream; proof size scaling verification.
- Integration tests: proof generation and verification on 100K-marker stream; concurrent proof generation during appends; external verifier example execution.
- Conformance tests: `tests/conformance/mmr_proof_verification.rs` -- normative proof verification tests using published vectors.
- Structured logs: `MMR_CHECKPOINT_UPDATED` (seq, mmr_root_prefix, trace_id), `MMR_INCLUSION_PROOF_GENERATED` (seq, proof_size, trace_id), `MMR_PREFIX_PROOF_GENERATED` (start_seq, end_seq, proof_size, trace_id), `MMR_PROOF_VERIFIED` (proof_type, result, trace_id), `MMR_DISABLED` (trace_id), `MMR_ENABLED` (rebuild_marker_count, trace_id).

## Expected Artifacts

- `src/control_plane/mmr_proofs.rs` -- MMR checkpoint and proof API implementation
- `tests/conformance/mmr_proof_verification.rs` -- normative conformance tests
- `artifacts/10.14/mmr_proof_vectors.json` -- published proof verification vectors
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1dar/verification_evidence.json` -- machine-readable CI/release gate evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1dar/verification_summary.md` -- human-readable verification summary

## Dependencies

- Upstream: bd-126h (append-only marker stream -- provides the hash-chained markers that the MMR accumulates).
- Downstream: bd-2ms (10.10 rollback/fork detection using MMR proofs), bd-3epz (section gate), bd-5rh (section gate).","acceptance_criteria":"MMR checkpoints can be enabled/disabled without corrupting marker truth; proof APIs verify inclusion and prefix claims; verifier examples pass.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CyanFinch","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:58.792991832Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T18:29:38.962242472Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T18:29:38.962207236Z","close_reason":"Completed implementation + artifacts; global validation blocked by upstream/shared issues","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-14","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1dar","depends_on_id":"bd-126h","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T16:24:19.037094863Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-1daz","title":"[10.14] Implement retroactive hardening pipeline that appends additional protection artifacts without rewriting canonical objects.","description":"## Why This Exists
When the system escalates its hardening level, objects that were created at a lower hardening level do not retroactively gain the additional protections of the new level. For example, an object created at `Standard` hardening lacks the extra integrity proofs that `Enhanced` hardening would have attached. Retroactive hardening solves this by appending additional protection artifacts (e.g., extra checksums, redundant parity, integrity proofs) to existing objects WITHOUT rewriting the canonical object data. This is the "union-only" principle — protection is additive, never destructive. The canonical object's identity (hash, ID, content) remains stable, but its protection envelope grows. Inspired by FrankenSQLite's retroactive WAL checksum strengthening (9J enhancement map), this supports Section 8.5 Invariant #8 (append-only protection — never rewrite to protect) and Invariant #4 (monotonic safety progression).

## What This Must Do
1. Implement `RetroactiveHardeningPipeline` in `crates/franken-node/src/policy/retroactive_hardening.rs` with:
   - `fn harden(object: &CanonicalObject, from_level: HardeningLevel, to_level: HardeningLevel) -> Vec<ProtectionArtifact>` — generates the additional protection artifacts needed to bring `object` from `from_level` to `to_level`.
   - `ProtectionArtifact` struct: `artifact_id`, `artifact_type: ProtectionType` (Checksum, Parity, IntegrityProof, RedundantCopy), `data: Vec<u8>`, `covers_object: ObjectId`, `hardening_level: HardeningLevel`.
   - `CanonicalObject` struct (or reference to existing): `object_id: ObjectId`, `content_hash: [u8; 32]`, `creation_level: HardeningLevel`.
2. Enforce the union-only principle:
   - The pipeline MUST NOT modify `CanonicalObject` content, hash, or ID.
   - Protection artifacts are stored alongside (not inside) canonical objects.
   - A verification function `fn verify_identity_stable(before: &CanonicalObject, after: &CanonicalObject) -> bool` confirms object identity is unchanged.
3. Make repairability improvement measurable:
   - `fn measure_repairability(object: &CanonicalObject, artifacts: &[ProtectionArtifact]) -> RepairabilityScore` — computes a score based on available protection.
   - Score is a numeric value (0.0 to 1.0) representing the fraction of object that is recoverable from protection artifacts alone.
4. Wire the pipeline into the hardening state machine's escalation path:
   - When the state machine escalates, the pipeline is invoked for all objects in a configurable target corpus.
   - Pipeline progress is recorded in the evidence ledger.
5. Write specification at `docs/specs/retroactive_hardening.md` documenting the union-only principle, protection artifact types, and repairability measurement methodology.
6. Write integration tests at `tests/integration/retroactive_hardening_union_only.rs` covering:
   - Object identity remains stable after hardening.
   - Protection artifacts are generated for the correct level gap.
   - Repairability score increases after hardening.
   - Empty corpus produces no artifacts (edge case).
7. Produce report at `artifacts/10.14/retroactive_hardening_report.json` with per-object repairability scores before and after hardening.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Retroactive hardening adds union-only artifacts; canonical object identity remains stable; repairability improvement is measurable on target corpus.
- `CanonicalObject.content_hash` is identical before and after retroactive hardening.
- `CanonicalObject.object_id` is identical before and after retroactive hardening.
- Protection artifacts are stored separately from canonical objects.
- Repairability score strictly increases (or stays at 1.0) after hardening.
- Pipeline handles multi-level gaps (e.g., Baseline -> Maximum in one operation).
- Report artifact shows before/after repairability for every object in test corpus.

## Testing & Logging Requirements
- Unit tests: Single-object hardening from each level to next; multi-level gap hardening; identity stability verification; repairability score calculation with known artifact set; empty corpus; already-at-max corpus (no new artifacts needed).
- Integration tests: Trigger escalation -> retroactive pipeline runs -> verify artifacts created -> verify identity stable -> verify repairability improved; concurrent hardening of multiple objects; pipeline progress appears in evidence ledger.
- Conformance tests: Union-only property — run hardening twice, second run produces no additional artifacts (idempotent); identity stability across 100 objects; repairability monotonicity across escalation sequence.
- Adversarial tests: Corrupt a canonical object between pipeline stages (verify detection); attempt to rewrite object via pipeline (verify rejection); object with maximum protection already (verify no-op); extremely large object corpus (10000 objects, verify bounded memory).
- Structured logs: `EVD-RETROHARDEN-001` on pipeline started (includes object count, from/to levels); `EVD-RETROHARDEN-002` on object hardened (includes `object_id`, artifacts created); `EVD-RETROHARDEN-003` on identity verification pass; `EVD-RETROHARDEN-004` on repairability score computed. All logs include `epoch_id`.

## Expected Artifacts
- `crates/franken-node/src/policy/retroactive_hardening.rs` — implementation
- `docs/specs/retroactive_hardening.md` — specification
- `tests/integration/retroactive_hardening_union_only.rs` — integration tests
- `artifacts/10.14/retroactive_hardening_report.json` — repairability report
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1daz/verification_evidence.json` — machine-readable pass/fail evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-1daz/verification_summary.md` — human-readable summary

## Dependencies
- Upstream: bd-3rya (hardening state machine — triggers retroactive hardening on escalation), bd-2e73 (evidence ledger — pipeline progress is recorded)
- Downstream: bd-1fp4 (integrity sweep scheduler uses retroactive hardening data), bd-3epz (section gate), bd-5rh (10.14 plan gate)","acceptance_criteria":"Retroactive hardening adds union-only artifacts; canonical object identity remains stable; repairability improvement is measurable on target corpus.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:56.299367956Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T19:37:45.578042125Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T19:37:45.578008132Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-14","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1daz","depends_on_id":"bd-2e73","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T16:23:53.258744062Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-1daz","depends_on_id":"bd-3rya","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T16:23:53.116082624Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-1djb2","title":"Reject partially tampered capability contracts at artifact admission","description":"Why this exists:
`crates/franken-node/src/extensions/artifact_contract.rs` currently computes the contract signature from `contract_id`, `extension_id`, `signer_id`, the capability count, and each `capability_id`, but it does not cover each capability's `scope` or `max_calls_per_epoch`. That means a caller can mutate the admitted capability envelope after signing, keep the original signature, and still pass `AdmissionGate::evaluate()`.

What this bug must do:
1. Bind contract signatures to the full signed capability envelope, not just capability ids.
2. Add focused regressions proving admission rejects contracts whose `scope` or `max_calls_per_epoch` are tampered after signing.
3. Preserve acceptance for unchanged valid contracts.
4. Run focused Rust validation through `rch` only.

Acceptance criteria:
1. `AdmissionGate::evaluate()` rejects a contract if any signed capability field changes after signing.
2. Existing valid signed contracts created by `make_contract()` still admit successfully.
3. Focused unit tests cover at least scope tampering and call-budget tampering.
4. Focused `rch`-offloaded checks/tests for `extensions::artifact_contract` pass.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"CrimsonReef","created_at":"2026-03-21T09:15:48.964519043Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T09:15:48.964519043Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["artifact-contract","extensions","security","signature-integrity"]}

That means a caller can mutate the admitted capability envelope after signing, keep the original signature, and still pass `AdmissionGate::evaluate()`.

What this bug must do:
1. Bind contract signatures to the full signed capability envelope, not just capability ids.
2. Add focused regressions proving admission rejects contracts whose `scope` or `max_calls_per_epoch` are tampered after signing.
3. Preserve acceptance for unchanged valid contracts.
4. Run focused Rust validation through `rch` only.

Acceptance criteria:

3. Focused unit tests cover at least scope tampering and call-budget tampering.
4. Focused `rch`-offloaded checks/tests for `extensions::artifact_contract` pass.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"CrimsonReef","created_at":"2026-03-21T09:15:48.964519043Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T09:42:49.589846174Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T09:42:49.589821578Z","close_reason":"Resolved on main by concurrent commit 0562977; rch cargo check/clippy passed, focused test runs blocked by remote frankenengine-engine SIGKILL","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["artifact-contract","extensions","security","signature-integrity"]}
 {"id":"bd-1dpd","title":"[PROGRAM] Enforce rch-only offload contract for CPU-intensive build/test/benchmark workflows","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md (Cross-cutting execution policy across Sections 10.0–10.21 and 11–16)

Task Objective:
Define and enforce a fail-closed execution policy requiring rch offload for CPU-intensive build/test/benchmark/coverage/e2e workloads so verification remains reproducible, resource-safe, and audit-traceable under parallel multi-agent execution.

Why This Exists:
The project explicitly requires CPU-intensive operations to run via rch. Without a dedicated policy bead and automated gate, agents may accidentally run heavy local cargo jobs, causing noisy contention, nondeterministic timing artifacts, and verification drift.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Publish canonical command-class policy that marks CPU-intensive workloads as rch-required (for example: cargo check, cargo clippy, cargo test, cargo nextest, cargo llvm-cov, benchmark suites, broad E2E sweeps).
- Define and enforce allowlist/exception process for commands that may run locally without violating policy.
- Implement fail-closed detector that flags local execution of rch-required workloads in CI/local verification gates.
- Emit machine-readable evidence proving each heavy verification run used rch and include worker provenance (worker id/host/queue/wait/run durations).
- Integrate policy checks into bootstrap, section, and program-wide verification gates.

Expected Artifacts:
- docs/verification/RCH_EXECUTION_POLICY.md
- schemas/rch_execution_provenance.schema.json
- scripts/verify_rch_required_workloads.sh
- artifacts/program/rch_execution_policy_report.json

Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for command classification logic, exception resolution, and policy parser behavior.
- E2E tests that run representative heavy workflows and assert fail-closed behavior when rch is bypassed.
- Detailed structured logs with stable event codes for classification decisions, policy violations, worker provenance, and remediation hints.
- Deterministic replay artifacts capturing command input, policy decision, and provenance bundle.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- Preserve full feature scope: this bead strengthens execution discipline only and must not weaken any existing verification/test requirements.
- This policy is additive to section/program gates and must be consumable without manual interpretation.
- Any bypass path must be explicit, logged, and policy-approved; silent bypass is forbidden.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- Prevents flaky or misleading verification results caused by uncontrolled local resource contention.
- Improves trust by making heavy verification runs auditable, reproducible, and attributable to specific offload workers.
- Reduces incident MTTR by pairing failures with deterministic provenance and clear policy-violation diagnostics.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ubuntu","created_at":"2026-02-20T08:23:55.611747103Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T08:32:49.126193776Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T08:32:49.126102376Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","program-integration","test-obligations","verification"]}
 {"id":"bd-1e0","title":"[10.9] Build migration singularity demo pipeline for flagship repositories.","description":"## [10.9] Build migration singularity demo pipeline for flagship repositories

### Why This Exists

Section 9H.1 (Migration Singularity Engine) and Section 9F.1 envision one-command migration from Node.js to franken_node as the ultimate adoption accelerator. If migration is painful, adoption stalls regardless of technical merit. This bead builds a demo pipeline that proves the concept on well-known, flagship Node.js repositories — the projects that every Node.js developer recognizes. A compelling demo showing Express, Fastify, or Next.js examples migrating with a single command (and producing verifiable before/after evidence) is the strongest possible category marketing asset.

### What It Must Do

Build an end-to-end migration demo pipeline that demonstrates one-command migration on flagship Node.js projects:

- **Target repositories**: The demo must work on at least three well-known Node.js projects spanning different categories: a web framework (Express or Fastify), a full-stack framework (Next.js starter or Remix example), and a utility library (lodash, date-fns, or equivalent). Repositories are pinned to specific versions for reproducibility.
- **Pipeline stages** (all automated, single command triggers full pipeline):
  1. **Audit**: Static analysis of the source repository identifying: API usage by compatibility band (safe/conditional/unsafe), dependency graph with risk annotations, platform-specific code patterns, and estimated migration complexity score.
  2. **Risk map**: Visual and structured output showing which code paths are safe for automatic migration, which require conditional handling, and which need manual intervention. Risk is quantified per file and per API call site.
  3. **Rewrite suggestions**: Automated code transformation suggestions for conditional and unsafe API usage. Suggestions include: the original code, the proposed replacement, confidence grade (high/medium/low), and rationale. High-confidence suggestions can be auto-applied; low-confidence suggestions are flagged for human review.
  4. **Validation**: After applying transformations, run the project's existing test suite under franken_node. Capture: tests passed, tests failed (with root cause classification), tests skipped (with reason).
  5. **Rollout plan**: A structured plan for deploying the migrated project, including: recommended rollout strategy (canary/blue-green/progressive), confidence grades per migration step, monitoring checkpoints, and rollback triggers.
- **Before/after evidence**: The pipeline produces side-by-side comparison artifacts: test pass rates before and after, performance benchmarks before and after, security posture before and after (trust verification, containment capabilities gained). Evidence is structured JSON with integrity hashes.
- **Reproducibility**: The entire pipeline runs in a hermetic container with pinned dependencies. An external party can execute `./migrate-demo.sh <repo-url> <version>` and reproduce the exact same results.
- **Category marketing output**: The pipeline produces a human-readable migration report (Markdown) suitable for publication, including: executive summary, detailed findings, before/after comparisons, and confidence assessment.

### Acceptance Criteria

1. Pipeline executes end-to-end on at least three flagship Node.js repositories with a single command.
2. All five pipeline stages (audit, risk map, rewrite suggestions, validation, rollout plan) produce structured output.
3. Rewrite suggestions include confidence grades; high-confidence suggestions are verifiably correct (validated by test suite).
4. Before/after evidence includes test pass rates, performance comparison, and security posture improvement.
5. Pipeline runs in a hermetic container and produces reproducible results across runs.
6. Migration report (Markdown) is generated automatically and is suitable for publication without manual editing.
7. The pipeline handles migration failures gracefully: partial migration produces a clear report of what succeeded, what failed, and recommended manual steps.
8. Execution time for the full pipeline on a medium-sized project (Express) is under 10 minutes on standard CI hardware.

### Key Dependencies

- Compatibility matrix from 10.2 for API risk band classification
- Migration system from 10.3 for rewrite transformation engine
- Benchmark infrastructure (bd-f5d) for performance comparison
- Trust verification infrastructure for security posture comparison

### Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests for each pipeline stage (audit, risk map, rewrite, validation, rollout plan).
- Integration test that runs the full pipeline on a small synthetic Node.js project.
- Verification script (`scripts/check_migration_demo.py`) with `--json` and `self_test()`.
- Pipeline execution logged at INFO with per-stage timing; failures logged at ERROR with root cause.

### Expected Artifacts

- `docs/specs/section_10_9/bd-1e0_contract.md` — migration demo specification
- `scripts/check_migration_demo.py` — verification script
- `tests/test_check_migration_demo.py` — unit tests
- `fixtures/migration-demos/` — pinned repository configurations
- `artifacts/section_10_9/bd-1e0/verification_evidence.json`
- `artifacts/section_10_9/bd-1e0/verification_summary.md`","acceptance_criteria":"1. Demo pipeline migrates at least 3 flagship open-source repositories (e.g., Express, Fastify, Next.js-like projects) from Node.js to franken_node with zero manual intervention.
2. Pipeline produces a migration singularity report: complete migration log, before/after test results, compatibility coverage percentage, and time-to-migrate.
3. Per Section 3 category targets: demo achieves >= 3x migration velocity compared to manual migration baseline for each flagship repo.
4. Pipeline is fully scripted and reproducible: scripts/run_singularity_demo.sh <repo-url> executes the entire migration and validation cycle.
5. Each demo includes a compatibility proof: full test suite of the migrated project passes under franken_node with results compared against Node.js baseline.
6. Pipeline handles failure gracefully: partial migrations produce a diagnostic report listing blocked modules, unsupported APIs, and suggested workarounds.
7. Demo artifacts (migration logs, test results, compatibility reports) are published under artifacts/ with a summary suitable for external stakeholders.","notes":"Plan-space QA addendum (2026-02-20):
1) Preserve full canonical-plan scope; no feature compression or silent omission is allowed.
2) Completion requires comprehensive verification coverage appropriate to scope: unit tests, integration tests, and E2E scripts/workflows with detailed structured logging (stable event/error codes + trace correlation IDs).
3) Completion requires reproducible evidence artifacts (machine-readable + human-readable) that allow independent replay and root-cause triage.
4) Any implementation PR for this bead must include explicit links to the above test/logging artifacts.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:48.445017898Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T23:32:33.215596213Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T23:32:33.215558533Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-9","self-contained","test-obligations"]}

+{"id":"bd-32big","title":"Fix unowned runtime clippy blockers in anti-entropy and optimization governor","description":"Why this exists:
During honest `rch` validation for `bd-woeq7`, `cargo clippy -p frankenengine-node --features extended-surfaces --lib -- -D warnings` failed on unowned runtime files outside the reserved session-auth lane.

Current concrete blockers in scope:
1. `crates/franken-node/src/runtime/anti_entropy.rs` triggers clippy `collapsible_if` in `TrustState::insert()`.
2. `crates/franken-node/src/runtime/optimization_governor.rs` triggers clippy `unnecessary_sort_by` in `OptimizationGovernor::live_check()`.

What this bug must do:
1. Clear the runtime clippy findings without reverting or trampling the existing dirty worktree changes already present in those files.
2. Preserve the current semantics of anti-entropy eviction precedence and live-check rollback ordering.
3. Run focused `rch` validation for the touched runtime surfaces only.

Acceptance criteria:
1. The targeted runtime clippy findings are eliminated.
2. Existing behavior remains intact, including precedence-based eviction and sequence-descending rollback.
3. Focused tests/checks through `rch` pass for the touched runtime surfaces.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"BlueDune","created_at":"2026-03-21T16:25:50.633756123Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T16:25:50.633756123Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["clippy","runtime","validation"]}

 {"id":"bd-32e","title":"Implement init command with profile bootstrapping","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md (Bootstrap bridge for Sections 10.0 and 10.1 operator onboarding)
Section: BOOTSTRAP (CLI onboarding bridge)

Task Objective:
Implement `franken-node init` with profile bootstrapping so first-time operators can create deterministic, policy-aligned project configuration safely.

In Scope:
- `init` command flow to generate baseline config/profile files.
- Deterministic profile template selection and safe overwrite semantics.
- Clear onboarding diagnostics for missing prerequisites and invalid target states.

Acceptance Criteria:
- `init` produces deterministic output files for equivalent inputs/options.
- Existing configuration handling is explicit (confirm/abort/backup semantics) and non-destructive by default.
- Generated profile artifacts are immediately consumable by downstream run/doctor flows.

Expected Artifacts:
- Init flow contract note with option matrix and file-output expectations.
- Generated fixture snapshots for representative init scenarios.
- Machine-readable init summary artifact for CI assertions.

- Machine-readable verification artifact at `artifacts/section_bootstrap/bd-32e/verification_evidence.json` for CI/release gating.
- Human-readable verification summary at `artifacts/section_bootstrap/bd-32e/verification_summary.md` linking implementation intent to measured outcomes.
Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for template rendering, path handling, and overwrite-policy logic.
- Integration tests validating end-to-end init behavior in clean and pre-existing directories.
- E2E tests simulating first-run operator onboarding workflows.
- Structured logs containing init phase events, file actions, and policy decisions with trace IDs.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- For "Implement init command with profile bootstrapping", preserve full capability scope from the plan; no feature compression or silent omission is allowed.
- Verification for "Implement init command with profile bootstrapping" must include capability-specific thresholds and machine-readable pass/fail evidence artifacts.
- Unit tests, integration/E2E scripts, and structured logs for "Implement init command with profile bootstrapping" must support deterministic replay and root-cause triage without hidden context.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- "Implement init command with profile bootstrapping" is required to reduce operator error, increase deterministic safety guarantees, and improve time-to-diagnosis for failures in real-world workflows.
- Explicit success/failure evidence for "Implement init command with profile bootstrapping" prevents false confidence and improves trust in migration/compatibility/security outcomes.
- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","notes":"Taking over bootstrap init implementation after bd-1pk closure","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PurpleHarbor","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:29:25.296726699Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:47:21.555255072Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T01:47:21.555227570Z","close_reason":"Completed: init bootstrap overwrite/backup policy + contract gate + evidence","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cli","config"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-32e","depends_on_id":"bd-3rp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:29:39.546835453Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-32e","depends_on_id":"bd-3vk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T08:04:16.558688526Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-32e","depends_on_id":"bd-n9r","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:29:39.613041834Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","acceptance_criteria":"1. At least 2 independent external parties (universities, security firms, or open-source organizations) have reproduced key claims.
2. Reproduced claims must include at least: (a) one compatibility claim (corpus pass rate), (b) one security claim (compromise reduction), (c) one performance claim (latency/throughput under hardening).
3. Each reproduction is documented with: reproducer identity, date, methodology, results, and delta from original claims.
4. Reproduction results are within 10% of original claims (allowing for environment differences).
5. Reproduction reports are published alongside project documentation.
6. Reproduction kit (instructions, data, scripts) is available in the public repository.
7. Evidence artifact: replication_registry.json listing each replication with party, claim, result, and date.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"MaroonFinch","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:39:35.213287458Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-21T01:28:21.552438955Z","closed_at":"2026-02-21T01:28:21.552410952Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-13","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-whxp","depends_on_id":"bd-2l1k","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:25.707874868Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-woeq7","title":"Reject duplicate live session IDs in session auth manager","description":"Why this exists:
`crates/franken-node/src/api/session_auth.rs` currently allows `SessionManager::establish_session()` to insert a new session over an existing live `session_id`. That silently overwrites the prior active session, resets send/recv sequence state, drops the old lifecycle without a termination event, and can bypass the intended monotonic/replay guarantees for that session identifier.

What this bug must do:
1. Reject establishment attempts that reuse a still-live session id instead of overwriting the existing session.
2. Preserve the ability to establish a new session once the old session is expired or terminated.
3. Add focused regressions proving duplicate live session ids are rejected and do not reset sequence state.
4. Run focused Rust validation through `rch` only.

Acceptance criteria:
1. `establish_session()` fails closed when the same `session_id` already exists in `Active`/`Establishing`/`Terminating` state.
2. Duplicate establishment does not replace the existing session or reset monotonic counters.
3. Re-establishing after expiration or termination still works when appropriate.
4. Focused `rch` checks/tests for `api::session_auth` pass.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-21T09:14:49.814868664Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T09:15:09.987583772Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"bd-woeq7","title":"Reject duplicate live session IDs in session auth manager","description":"Why this exists:
`crates/franken-node/src/api/session_auth.rs` currently allows `SessionManager::establish_session()` to insert a new session over an existing live `session_id`. That silently overwrites the prior active session, resets send/recv sequence state, drops the old lifecycle without a termination event, and can bypass the intended monotonic/replay guarantees for that session identifier.

What this bug must do:
1. Reject establishment attempts that reuse a still-live session id instead of overwriting the existing session.
2. Preserve the ability to establish a new session once the old session is expired or terminated.
3. Add focused regressions proving duplicate live session ids are rejected and do not reset sequence state.
4. Run focused Rust validation through `rch` only.

Acceptance criteria:
1. `establish_session()` fails closed when the same `session_id` already exists in `Active`/`Establishing`/`Terminating` state.
2. Duplicate establishment does not replace the existing session or reset monotonic counters.
3. Re-establishing after expiration or termination still works when appropriate.
4. Focused `rch` checks/tests for `api::session_auth` pass.","notes":"Patch landed in crates/franken-node/src/api/session_auth.rs.

Validation status:
- rch cargo check -j 1 -p frankenengine-node --features extended-surfaces --all-targets: PASS
- rustfmt --edition 2024 --check crates/franken-node/src/api/session_auth.rs: PASS
- rch cargo clippy -p frankenengine-node --features extended-surfaces --lib -- -D warnings: BLOCKED by unrelated existing warnings/errors in crates/franken-node/src/api/operator_routes.rs plus unrelated clippy findings in runtime/anti_entropy.rs and runtime/optimization_governor.rs
- focused rch cargo test for new session_auth regressions: still not returning a definitive result on remote workers; treating as infra noise until proven otherwise

Keep in progress until either focused test returns cleanly or external workspace blockers are cleared.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-21T09:14:49.814868664Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T09:55:40.841919025Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-wpck","title":"[15] Pillar: migration kit ecosystem","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md
Section: 15

Task Objective:
Build migration kit ecosystem for major Node/Bun archetypes.

Execution Requirements:
- Make this requirement machine-verifiable and release-gated where applicable.
- Keep behavior self-documenting so future contributors do not need to re-open the master plan.

Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for rule/contract logic and error conditions.
- Integration/E2E tests for workflow-level enforcement.
- Structured logs with stable codes and trace IDs.
- Reproducible artifacts that prove contract satisfaction.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Success and failure invariants for [15] Pillar: migration kit ecosystem are explicitly quantified and machine-verifiable.
- Determinism requirements for [15] Pillar: migration kit ecosystem are validated across repeated runs and adversarial perturbations.


Expected Artifacts:
- Machine-readable verification artifact with explicit canonical path under artifacts/section_15/bd-wpck/verification_evidence.json, suitable for CI/release gating.
- Human-readable verification summary with explicit canonical path under artifacts/section_15/bd-wpck/verification_summary.md, linking implementation intent to measured validation outcomes.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- The implementation must deliver the exact capability named in the title, with no scope dilution and no silent feature omission.
- Validation artifacts must include capability-specific thresholds, pass/fail criteria, and reproducible evidence bundles tied to this bead ID.
- Program/section verification gates must be able to consume this bead's outputs without manual interpretation.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- "[15] Pillar: migration kit ecosystem" is required to reduce operator error, increase deterministic safety guarantees, and improve time-to-diagnosis for failures in real-world workflows.
- Explicit success/failure evidence for "[15] Pillar: migration kit ecosystem" prevents false confidence and improves trust in migration/compatibility/security outcomes.
- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Migration kits exist for >= 5 major Node.js/Bun archetypes: (a) Express.js/Fastify web server, (b) Next.js/Remix SSR app, (c) CLI tool (Commander/Yargs), (d) npm library package, (e) background worker (Bull/BullMQ queue processor).
2. Each kit includes: (a) automated analyzer that scores migration readiness, (b) migration script that handles >= 80% of steps automatically, (c) manual intervention guide for remaining steps, (d) post-migration validation suite, (e) rollback script.
3. Kits are published as npm packages installable via 'npx franken-migrate --kit <archetype>'.
4. Each kit has been validated on >= 3 real-world projects of its archetype (documented in test matrix).
5. Kit documentation includes: estimated migration time, known limitations, and compatibility exceptions.
6. Bun-specific kits exist for >= 2 archetypes with documented Bun-specific compatibility delta.
7. Evidence: migration_kit_matrix.json with per-archetype kit status, validated projects, and success rates.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:39:36.246627380Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-21T06:09:22.922570077Z","closed_at":"2026-02-21T06:09:22.922545932Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-15","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-wpck","depends_on_id":"bd-209w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:26.255430105Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

Benchmark suite includes security co-metrics alongside performance metrics: (a) attack surface area (number of exposed APIs under test profile), (b) containment latency under attack, (c) trust decision accuracy under adversarial load.
2. Trust co-metrics include: (a) false positive rate of trust decisions, (b) false negative rate (malicious extensions passing trust checks), (c) trust decision latency p50/p95/p99.
3. Security and trust metrics are reported in the same benchmark output as performance metrics (unified report).
4. Co-metric baselines are established: each security/trust metric has a defined acceptable range.
5. Trade-off analysis: report explicitly shows performance vs security trade-offs (e.g., 'strict profile: +20ms p99 latency, -90% attack surface').
6. Co-metrics are measured under at least 2 hardening profiles (balanced, strict).
7.
